Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Heinrich Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Heinrich Pte Ltd is a company controlled by Kor Yong Koo.

A written joint venture agreement dated 2006-10-18 described an interest-free shareholders' loan of up to S$300,000 from Kor Yong Koo to Heinrich for working capital, to be disbursed over 24 months from 2006-10-15.

Under the 2006 joint venture agreement, if the S$300,000 loan was not fully repaid within three years, each of Lau Kim Huat, Lim Keng Leng and Yasmin Binte Mustaffah would be responsible for 10% of the outstanding loan amount.

By April 2009, some of the loans from Kor Yong Koo to Heinrich had been converted into share capital to pay for allotment of shares to Lau Kim Huat and Kor Yong Koo, reducing the outstanding loan balance.

A directors' resolution in or around April 2009 approved capitalisation of loan amounts into share capital in Heinrich, resulting in the issue of 29,999 shares to Lau Kim Huat, bringing his total shareholding to 30,000 shares, representing 10% of Heinrich's share capital.
Between 2006-10-18 and 2009-10-18, Heinrich's general ledgers recorded payments totalling approximately S$494,066.63 as amounts paid out to or for the account of Lau Kim Huat.
In 2006, the business of Macmacor Engineering was acquired by Kor Yong Koo, and the business was later incorporated as Macmacor Engineering Pte Ltd.
In the first year after the acquisition of the Macmacor business in 2006, Macmacor recorded a profit while experiencing a shortage of funds to increase stocks and sales.
Around 2006, the business of Macmacor Engineering was described to Kor Yong Koo as having good business but being short of funds to expand its stocks and sales.
Around 2006, the sales level of Macmacor Engineering was discussed with reference to an annual figure close to S$1 million.
From about 2006, Heinrich Pte Ltd carried on a business providing container lashing services.

From about 2006, Heinrich's warehouse, forklifts, employees and freight forwarder were used in handling goods that were delivered to Heinrich's premises and later exported to a company in China associated with Li Cunkou.
From about 2006 onwards, Heinrich's resources were used on multiple occasions to arrange courier shipments and transhipments of items connected with Li Cunkou and his businesses.

From about 2006 onwards, Heinrich's staff, including its director Lau Kim Huat, were involved in sourcing products and arranging delivery of items requested by or for the benefit of Li Cunkou and his related companies.

Heinrich's warehouse and equipment were used to store and handle coils of ropes supplied by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd after an attempted sale to Gaylin International Pte Ltd did not proceed.

After the attempted sale of ropes to Gaylin International Pte Ltd did not proceed, one coil of rope stored at Heinrich's premises was sold to Transvictory Marine and Offshore Pte Ltd for a profit of about S$4,500, and another coil was sold to PT Bumi Aranda for a profit of about S$1,000.
Seven coils of rope stored at Heinrich's premises were sold to Impa Marine Pte Ltd in two transactions, and Impa Marine Pte Ltd later resold those ropes at a profit.

In connection with the sale of ropes to Impa Marine Pte Ltd, cheques issued by Impa were encashed and the proceeds were handled through payment vouchers that recorded cash payments to an individual identified as 'Ah Siong'.
An employee of Heinrich Pte Ltd named Lin Yong Siang was known within the company by the nickname 'Ah Siong'.
Payment vouchers prepared within Heinrich in relation to rope transactions described the source of certain ropes as a 'scrap supplier'.
Heinrich's funds were used to pay courier charges for shipments connected with Li Cunkou, including shipments of personal effects.
Import permits were submitted in relation to courier shipments arranged through Heinrich for items connected with Li Cunkou.
By 2012, the working relationship between Kor Yong Koo and Lau Kim Huat had deteriorated, and around that time Lau and Li Cunkou discussed starting a new business venture together.

While still employed at Heinrich, preparations were made for the establishment of Hai Yuan Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd, including printing name cards and arranging Goods and Services Tax registration for Hai Yuan.
While still working at Heinrich, information relating to Heinrich's business was accessed and later not retained on Heinrich's office computer after Lau Kim Huat left the company.
In 2013, Lau Kim Huat left Heinrich Pte Ltd and subsequently set up a new company with Li Cunkou.
After Lau Kim Huat left Heinrich in 2013, Kor Yong Koo reviewed past transactions and activities involving Lau, Li Cunkou and JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial arrangements, the defendants' use of Heinrich Pte Ltd's funds, facilities, staff time and transactional structures resulted in uncompensated use of Heinrich's resources or financial shortfall under the 2006 joint venture loan and Macmacor acquisition.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and related parties are generally manage company funds and resources in accordance with agreed financing terms, to document and account for transactions involving company assets, and to ensure that third parties who use company facilities or staff time provide appropriate value or reimbursement.
Party_Details.Application: The 2006 joint venture agreement fixed an interest-free loan framework of S$300,000 with a three-year reference period and specified percentage responsibilities for named individuals if the loan remained outstanding. Heinrich's ledgers recorded more than S$300,000 in payments credited to or for the account of Lau Kim Huat within that three-year window, and a directors' resolution in April 2009 capitalised loan amounts into share capital, issuing 29,999 shares to Lau and bringing his stake to 10%. In parallel, Heinrich's warehouse, forklifts, employees and freight forwarder were used over several years to handle ropes and other goods connected with JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd and Li Cunkou, and Heinrich funds were used for courier and transhipment activities. Some of these activities generated identifiable profits for Heinrich, such as profits on rope sales to Transvictory Marine and Offshore Pte Ltd and PT Bumi Aranda, and resale profits by Impa Marine Pte Ltd, while other activities primarily consumed staff time and facilities without clear direct revenue attribution. These patterns frame the quantitative relationship between the loan flows, share capitalisation, and the extent of uncompensated or indirectly compensated use of Heinrich's operational resources by counterparties linked to the defendants.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge concluded that the JV Agreement covered only the initial S$300,000 loan, that this amount had been repaid within the three-year period as evidenced by ledger entries and the subsequent share capitalisation, and that Lau's liability under the JV Agreement, even if engaged, would be limited to 10% of that S$300,000. The court held that the plaintiffs did not prove actionable misrepresentation in relation to the Macmacor acquisition because the key statements were either true, non-factual opinions, not shown to have been made, or not shown to have been relied upon by Kor, and the Misrepresentation Act did not apply as there was no contract between Kor and Lau. On the directors' duties claims, the court applied the Intraco and Ho Kang Peng standards and found that Lau's activities, including rope transactions, transhipments, courier arrangements and other assistance to Li, were commercially explicable as efforts to cultivate a supplier and did not amount to acting otherwise than bona fide in Heinrich's interests, nor did they demonstrate conflicts of interest, improper purposes, or misuse of position or information under s 157 of the Companies Act. The court found no loss to Heinrich from the rope transactions, accepted that Heinrich made profits on onward sales, and rejected the suggestion of secret profits or sham payments, accepting evidence that 'Ah Siong' referred to an actual employee and that the description 'scrap supplier' on vouchers did not indicate wrongdoing. The cumulative scale of Lau's activities for Li was held insufficient to infer disloyalty or a shift of allegiance, and the later formation of a business with Li in 2012–2013 was not treated as evidence that earlier conduct was disloyal. Because no breach of fiduciary or statutory duty by Lau was established, the knowing assistance claims against Li and JHY failed at the threshold; in any event, the court held that the plaintiffs had not shown the dishonest state of mind required for knowing assistance, as there was no evidence that Li or JHY knew of any breach or acted contrary to ordinary standards of honesty. The court further held that the 'user principle' is a measure of damages contingent on an underlying wrong involving use of property and does not create a free-standing cause of action; since no primary wrong was established and the claims concerned use of services and facilities rather than wrongful use of property in the relevant sense, the user-based restitutionary claim could not succeed. Overall, the court characterised the plaintiffs' approach as a wide-ranging, ex post attempt to recast many years of minor and low-value transactions as misconduct following Lau's departure, and determined that suspicion and disappointment did not translate into legal liability. All claims were dismissed and costs of S$100,000 plus reasonable disbursements were awarded to the defendants.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Kor Yong Koo

Facts.Text: From about 1995, Kor Yong Koo and Lau Kim Huat knew each other and worked together.
Around 2006, after discussions with Lau Kim Huat, Kor Yong Koo agreed to establish Heinrich Pte Ltd to carry on a container lashing business.

On 2006-10-18, Kor Yong Koo entered into a written joint venture agreement with Lau Kim Huat, Lim Keng Leng, Yasmin Binte Mustaffah and Heinrich Pte Ltd, under which Kor agreed to provide an interest-free shareholders' loan of up to S$300,000 to Heinrich.
Pursuant to the 2006 joint venture agreement, Kor Yong Koo provided funds to Heinrich up to a total of S$300,000 as an interest-free shareholders' loan for working capital.
By 2009, Kor Yong Koo had advanced further sums to Heinrich beyond the initial S$300,000, bringing the total loans from him to about S$1.3 million.
In or around April 2009, part of the loans from Kor Yong Koo to Heinrich were converted into share capital, including S$29,999 used to pay for a 10% allotment of shares to Lau Kim Huat.
Kor Yong Koo waived a condition that required repayment of loans before share allotment, allowing shares in Heinrich to be allotted to Lau Kim Huat while loans remained outstanding.
In 2006, Kor Yong Koo acquired the business of Macmacor Engineering and later arranged for its incorporation as Macmacor Engineering Pte Ltd.
In 2006, before acquiring the Macmacor Engineering business, Kor Yong Koo discussed the business with Lau Kim Huat, including its profitability, sales level and fit with Heinrich's operations.
Following the acquisition of Macmacor Engineering in 2006, Kor Yong Koo observed that Macmacor was profitable in its first year but lacked funds to increase stock and sales.

Around the time of the Macmacor acquisition in 2006, Kor Yong Koo considered statements about Macmacor being a good business to acquire, being profitable, complementing Heinrich, and having sales close to S$1 million a year.
By 2012, the relationship between Kor Yong Koo and Lau Kim Huat had deteriorated, and Kor became dissatisfied with Lau's conduct in relation to Heinrich's affairs.
After Lau Kim Huat left Heinrich in 2013 to start a new company with Li Cunkou, Kor Yong Koo reviewed past transactions and activities involving Lau, Li and JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd.

In 2013, following his review of past dealings, Kor Yong Koo initiated legal proceedings in the High Court of Singapore as Suit No 770 of 2013 against Lau Kim Huat, Li Cunkou and JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the pre-dispute financial flows and information available to him, Kor Yong Koo experienced uncompensated loan exposure and adverse economic effects from the Macmacor acquisition and from Heinrich's dealings with entities connected to the defendants.
Party_Details.Rule: An investor or shareholder who advances funds and acquires businesses typically evaluates repayment patterns, capitalisation decisions, and business performance data to determine whether counterparties have met agreed financial responsibilities and whether acquisitions and related-party dealings have produced the expected economic benefits.
Party_Details.Application: Kor advanced S$300,000 under the 2006 joint venture loan framework and later increased his total loans to Heinrich to about S$1.3 million, while also waiving a condition that loans be repaid before share allotment, enabling a 10% share issue to Lau funded by S$29,999 of Kor's loan. Ledger entries showed substantial repayments credited to Lau, and a directors' resolution in April 2009 capitalised loan amounts into share capital. In parallel, Kor acquired the Macmacor Engineering business in 2006 after discussions with Lau about profitability, sales near S$1 million, and complementarity with Heinrich, and later observed that Macmacor was profitable in its first year but short of funds to expand. Over the following years, Heinrich's facilities and staff were used in transactions involving JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd and Li Cunkou, some of which generated profits for Heinrich and some of which primarily consumed resources. These pre-dispute data points define Kor's exposure: the scale of his loans relative to recorded repayments and capitalisation, the actual performance of Macmacor compared with the described prospects, and the net economic effect on Heinrich of the resource-intensive dealings with entities linked to the defendants.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Kor's claims as co-extensive with Heinrich's and analysed his position as lender, shareholder and acquirer of Macmacor. It held that the JV Agreement did not extend beyond the initial S$300,000 loan and that this loan had been repaid, undermining Kor's contention that Lau remained responsible for a larger outstanding amount or for 30% of a broader loan pool. On the Macmacor acquisition, the judge found that Kor did not prove that he relied on Lau's statements in the legally relevant sense, noting that Kor was an experienced businessman who could and did consult others, and that the alleged misrepresentations were either true, non-actionable opinions, or not shown to have been made or relied upon. The court emphasised the long delay between the 2006 acquisition and the 2013 complaint, and the absence of contemporaneous protest, as factors weakening Kor's narrative. Regarding the extensive list of alleged breaches of duty and misuses of Heinrich's resources, the judge concluded that Kor's post-2013 review of historical transactions reflected disappointment and a sense of betrayal after Lau left to join Li, but that this retrospective dissatisfaction did not establish legal wrongs. The court described Kor's approach as a 'scatter-shot' or 'kitchen-sink' strategy that obscured the truly material issues, and ultimately held that none of the pleaded causes of action were made out on the evidence, resulting in dismissal of Kor's personal and derivative claims.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lau Kim Huat

Facts.Text: From about 1995, Lau Kim Huat and Kor Yong Koo knew each other and had worked together.
Around 2006, Lau Kim Huat began working for Heinrich Pte Ltd and acted as a director of the company.
In 2006, Lau Kim Huat proposed to Kor Yong Koo the establishment of Heinrich Pte Ltd to operate a container lashing business, involving himself, Lim Keng Leng and Yasmin Binte Mustaffah.

On 2006-10-18, Lau Kim Huat signed a joint venture agreement with Kor Yong Koo, Lim Keng Leng, Yasmin Binte Mustaffah and Heinrich Pte Ltd, under which Kor would provide an interest-free shareholders' loan of up to S$300,000 to Heinrich.

Under the 2006 joint venture agreement, Lau Kim Huat agreed that if the S$300,000 loan was not fully repaid within three years, he would be responsible for 10% of any outstanding amount, alongside similar 10% responsibilities for Lim Keng Leng and Yasmin Binte Mustaffah.
Between 2006-10-18 and 2009-10-18, Heinrich's general ledgers recorded payments totalling approximately S$494,066.63 as amounts credited to or for the account of Lau Kim Huat.

In or around April 2009, a directors' resolution of Heinrich Pte Ltd approved the capitalisation of loan amounts into share capital, resulting in the issue of 29,999 shares to Lau Kim Huat and bringing his total shareholding to 30,000 shares, representing 10% of Heinrich's share capital.
In April 2009, S$29,999 of loan funds provided by Kor Yong Koo were applied towards payment for a 10% allotment of shares in Heinrich to Lau Kim Huat.
Around 2006, Lau Kim Huat introduced the owner of Macmacor Engineering to Kor Yong Koo in connection with Kor's potential acquisition of the Macmacor business.

In discussions around 2006, Lau Kim Huat described Macmacor Engineering to Kor Yong Koo as a business with good prospects that was short of funds to expand, and referred to its profitability and sales level.
Around 2006, Lau Kim Huat conveyed to Kor Yong Koo that Macmacor Engineering's sales were close to S$1 million a year, based on information he had received from the then owner of Macmacor.

Around 2006, Lau Kim Huat expressed the view to Kor Yong Koo that Macmacor Engineering was a good business to acquire, that it would complement Heinrich's business, and that its sales could increase after an injection of funds.

From about 2006 onwards, Lau Kim Huat arranged for goods purchased by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd from suppliers in the United Kingdom and the United States to be delivered to Heinrich's warehouse, where Heinrich's employees and equipment handled the unloading and subsequent export of the goods to a company in China associated with Li Cunkou.
From about 2006 onwards, Lau Kim Huat used Heinrich's usual freight forwarder to manage transhipment of goods connected with JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd and Li Cunkou.
From about 2006 onwards, Lau Kim Huat arranged courier shipments using Heinrich's accounts for items requested by or for the benefit of Li Cunkou, including shipments of personal effects.
From about 2006 onwards, Lau Kim Huat sourced products and samples at the request of Li Cunkou or his company in China and arranged for these products or samples to be sent to China.

In connection with the attempted sale of ropes supplied by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd to Gaylin International Pte Ltd, Lau Kim Huat approached Gaylin as a potential buyer and arranged for the ropes to be offered to Gaylin.

After Gaylin International Pte Ltd did not proceed with the rope purchase, Lau Kim Huat stored nine coils of rope at Heinrich's premises at the request of Li Cunkou and sought buyers for the ropes on a consignment basis.

Following the aborted Gaylin transaction, Lau Kim Huat arranged the sale of one coil of rope to Transvictory Marine and Offshore Pte Ltd for a profit of about S$4,500 and another coil to PT Bumi Aranda for a profit of about S$1,000.
Lau Kim Huat arranged the sale of seven coils of rope to Impa Marine Pte Ltd in two transactions, after which Impa Marine Pte Ltd resold the ropes at a profit.

In relation to the rope sales to Impa Marine Pte Ltd, Lau Kim Huat signed payment vouchers and encashed cheques issued by Impa, with the vouchers recording cash payments to an individual identified as 'Ah Siong'.
On payment vouchers prepared in Heinrich for rope-related payments, Lau Kim Huat described the source of certain ropes as a 'scrap supplier'.
In arranging courier shipments for items connected with Li Cunkou, Lau Kim Huat used Heinrich's funds to pay courier charges and submitted import permits that described the contents of the shipments.
By 2012, after the relationship with Kor Yong Koo had deteriorated, Lau Kim Huat discussed with Li Cunkou the possibility of starting a new business venture together.

While still employed at Heinrich, Lau Kim Huat arranged for the printing of name cards and Goods and Services Tax registration for Hai Yuan Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd in preparation for his subsequent role there.
In 2013, Lau Kim Huat left his position at Heinrich Pte Ltd and subsequently set up a new company with Li Cunkou.
Before leaving Heinrich in 2013, Lau Kim Huat deleted emails relating to transactions with Li Cunkou and JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd from his office computer.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on his pre-dispute conduct and financial dealings, Lau Kim Huat's management of loan repayments, share capitalisation, Macmacor-related information, and Heinrich's resources in transactions involving Li Cunkou and JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd resulted in uncompensated use of Heinrich's assets or shortfall under agreed financial arrangements.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who participates in shareholder loan arrangements, facilitates acquisitions, and controls the use of company facilities and funds is generally ensure that loan obligations are satisfied in accordance with written terms, that share issuances reflect actual capital contributions, and that third-party use of company resources is structured so that the company either earns a profit or receives appropriate value for the resources deployed.
Party_Details.Application: Under the 2006 joint venture agreement, Lau accepted a defined 10% responsibility for any outstanding portion of the S$300,000 loan if it remained unpaid after three years. Heinrich's ledgers recorded more than S$300,000 in amounts credited to or for Lau between 2006-10-18 and 2009-10-18, and a directors' resolution in April 2009 capitalised loan amounts into share capital, issuing 29,999 shares to Lau funded by S$29,999 of Kor's loan. In parallel, Lau introduced the Macmacor Engineering opportunity to Kor and conveyed information about its profitability, sales level and fit with Heinrich, after which Kor acquired the business and Macmacor recorded a profit in its first year while being short of funds to expand. Over several years, Lau arranged for Heinrich's warehouse, forklifts, employees and freight forwarder to handle goods purchased by JHY from overseas suppliers and exported to a company in China associated with Li, and used Heinrich's funds and courier accounts for shipments and sourcing activities requested by Li. Some of these activities generated identifiable profits for Heinrich, such as profits on rope sales to Transvictory and PT Bumi Aranda and sales to Impa, while others primarily consumed staff time and facilities. These pre-dispute patterns define the quantitative and operational context for assessing whether Lau's management of repayments, capitalisation and resource deployment left Heinrich without appropriate compensation or exposed Kor's loan position beyond what the written JV terms contemplated.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the JV Agreement imposed on Lau only a 10% exposure to the defined S$300,000 'Loan' and rejected the plaintiffs' attempt to extend his responsibility to later advances totalling S$1.3 million. By construing the contract's wording and examining the ledgers and the April 2009 capitalisation, the judge concluded that the S$300,000 had in fact been repaid within the three-year period and that the share issue to Lau was consistent with completion of the bargain. On the Macmacor acquisition, the judge found that the plaintiffs did not prove that Lau made fraudulent or negligent misstatements or that Kor relied on them; the court emphasised that Macmacor was actually profitable in its first year, that statements about complementarity and future growth were opinions, and that the Misrepresentation Act did not apply because Lau was not a party to the acquisition contract. Regarding directors' duties, the court applied the Intraco and Ho Kang Peng framework and determined that Lau's actions in arranging rope transactions, transhipments, courier shipments and other assistance to Li were commercially explicable as efforts to cultivate a supplier and did not cross the threshold into disloyalty, conflict of interest, improper purpose or lack of honesty under s 157 of the Companies Act. The judge noted that Heinrich was not shown to have suffered loss on the rope transactions, that it in fact made profits on onward sales, and that there was no evidence of secret profits. The court accepted explanations for the use of 'Ah Siong' on vouchers and the 'scrap supplier' description, and found that the cumulative scale of Lau's activities for Li was insufficient to infer a shift of allegiance away from Heinrich. The later decision to form a new company with Li in 2012–2013 was not treated as evidence that earlier conduct was disloyal. The court also rejected the plaintiffs' reliance on the deletion of emails as a basis for adverse inferences. Overall, the judge concluded that Lau's conduct did not amount to breach of fiduciary, statutory or common law duties and that the claims against him failed.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Li Cunkou

Facts.Text: From about 2006, Li Cunkou was an associate of Lau Kim Huat and was involved in marine and offshore equipment business activities.
Li Cunkou owned or controlled JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd, a company that supplied ropes and other goods.

From about 2006 onwards, Li Cunkou arranged for JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd to purchase goods from suppliers in the United Kingdom and the United States and to have those goods delivered to Heinrich Pte Ltd's warehouse in Singapore.

From about 2006 onwards, goods purchased by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd for Li Cunkou's business in China were unloaded and handled at Heinrich's premises using Heinrich's employees, forklifts and freight forwarder, and then exported to a company in China associated with Li.
Around 2006, Li Cunkou supplied nine coils of rope through JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd for an attempted sale to Gaylin International Pte Ltd arranged by Lau Kim Huat.

After Gaylin International Pte Ltd did not proceed with the rope purchase, Li Cunkou requested that the nine coils of rope remain stored at Heinrich's premises and that Lau Kim Huat sell the ropes on consignment.

Following the aborted Gaylin transaction, ropes supplied by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd for Li Cunkou were sold to Transvictory Marine and Offshore Pte Ltd, PT Bumi Aranda and Impa Marine Pte Ltd, generating profits for Heinrich and for JHY or Li.
From about 2006 onwards, Li Cunkou requested that Lau Kim Huat source various products and samples, including non-marine items such as healthcare products, and arrange for these to be sent to China.
From about 2006 onwards, Li Cunkou requested that Lau Kim Huat arrange courier shipments using Heinrich's accounts for items connected with Li's business and personal matters.
From about 2006 onwards, Li Cunkou engaged Lau Kim Huat to assist with various personal matters, including arranging repair of furniture and shipment of personal effects, using Heinrich's resources.
By 2012, after the relationship between Kor Yong Koo and Lau Kim Huat had deteriorated, Li Cunkou discussed with Lau the possibility of starting a new business venture together.
Around 2012, preparations were made for the establishment of Hai Yuan Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd, a company in which Li Cunkou and Lau Kim Huat intended to be involved.
In 2013, after Lau Kim Huat left Heinrich Pte Ltd, Li Cunkou and Lau set up a new company together to carry on marine and offshore equipment business.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute transactions and interactions, Li Cunkou's use of Heinrich Pte Ltd's warehouse, staff, freight services and courier facilities through JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd and personal requests resulted in uncompensated or under-compensated use of Heinrich's resources.
Party_Details.Rule: A business counterparty who routes goods and personal or business shipments through another company's facilities and staff is generally ensure that the host company is appropriately compensated through pricing, commissions or other consideration for storage, handling, sourcing and administrative services.
Party_Details.Application: From 2006 onwards, Li used Heinrich's warehouse, forklifts, employees and freight forwarder to handle goods purchased by JHY from overseas suppliers and exported to a company in China associated with him, and he requested that nine coils of rope be stored at Heinrich and sold on consignment after the Gaylin transaction did not proceed. These ropes were later sold to Transvictory, PT Bumi Aranda and Impa, generating profits for both Heinrich and JHY or Li. In addition, Li requested product sourcing, sample shipments and courier arrangements using Heinrich's accounts for both business and personal matters, consuming staff time and incurring courier charges. The record shows that some of these activities produced identifiable profits or potential supply relationships for Heinrich, while others primarily involved service provision without clearly documented direct charges. These pre-dispute patterns define the extent to which Li's business and personal dealings leveraged Heinrich's infrastructure and whether the economic return to Heinrich matched the level of resources deployed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the knowing assistance claim against Li required proof of an underlying breach of fiduciary duty by Lau and a dishonest state of mind on Li's part. Having found no breach by Lau, the court concluded that the claim against Li failed at the outset. In the alternative, the judge examined Li's conduct and found no evidence that Li knew or ought to have known that Lau was acting in breach of duty; the fact that Li requested transhipments, storage, sourcing and courier services through Heinrich did not, in the court's view, show that Li appreciated any irregularity or conflict. The judge emphasised that the plaintiffs had not demonstrated the level of knowledge or dishonesty required under the Royal Brunei and George Raymond Zage line of authorities, and that nothing in Li's conduct, including the later decision to form a new company with Lau, met the standard of acting contrary to ordinary standards of honest behaviour. The court also rejected the plaintiffs' attempt to invoke the user principle against Li, holding that it is a measure of damages contingent on an established wrong involving use of property and that no such underlying wrong was proven. Accordingly, all claims against Li were dismissed.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd is a company belonging to or controlled by Li Cunkou that trades in ropes and other marine and offshore equipment.
From about 2006, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd had a business relationship with Heinrich Pte Ltd through Lau Kim Huat and Li Cunkou.

From about 2006 onwards, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd purchased goods from suppliers in the United Kingdom and the United States and arranged for those goods to be delivered to Heinrich Pte Ltd's warehouse in Singapore for handling and onward export.

From about 2006 onwards, goods purchased by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd were unloaded and handled at Heinrich's premises using Heinrich's employees, forklifts and freight forwarder, and then exported to a company in China associated with Li Cunkou.
Around 2006, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd supplied nine coils of rope for an attempted sale to Gaylin International Pte Ltd arranged through Heinrich and Lau Kim Huat.
After Gaylin International Pte Ltd did not proceed with the rope purchase, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd left nine coils of rope at Heinrich's premises for storage and consignment sale.

Following the aborted Gaylin transaction, coils of rope supplied by JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd were sold to Transvictory Marine and Offshore Pte Ltd, PT Bumi Aranda and Impa Marine Pte Ltd, generating profits for Heinrich and for JHY or its owner.

In connection with the rope transactions, Heinrich made payments to JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd within a short period after receiving sale proceeds, while some other Heinrich suppliers experienced longer payment intervals.
From about 2006 onwards, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd used Heinrich's facilities and staff time in multiple transhipment and sourcing transactions arranged by Lau Kim Huat.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute dealings, JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd's use of Heinrich Pte Ltd's warehouse, staff, freight services and payment flows in rope and other goods transactions resulted in uncompensated or under-compensated use of Heinrich's resources.
Party_Details.Rule: A supplier that stores goods at another company's premises, uses its staff and logistics infrastructure, and receives payments through that company is generally ensure that the host company is compensated through commissions, margins or other agreed terms for storage, handling and transactional support.
Party_Details.Application: From 2006 onwards, JHY used Heinrich's warehouse, forklifts, employees and freight forwarder to handle goods purchased from overseas suppliers and exported to a company in China associated with its owner, and it stored nine coils of rope at Heinrich for consignment sale after the Gaylin transaction did not proceed. These ropes were later sold to Transvictory, PT Bumi Aranda and Impa, with Heinrich earning profits on some sales and JHY or its owner receiving sale proceeds via payments from Heinrich. Heinrich's payment timing to JHY on rope transactions was relatively prompt compared with some other suppliers, and JHY's use of Heinrich's facilities and staff time extended over multiple transactions. These pre-dispute patterns define the extent to which JHY's business leveraged Heinrich's infrastructure and whether the economic return to Heinrich, via margins or commissions, matched the level of resources deployed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the knowing assistance claim against JHY depended on establishing a breach of fiduciary duty by Lau and attributing to JHY a dishonest state of mind through its directing mind, Li. Having found no breach by Lau and no dishonesty on Li's part, the court concluded that the claim against JHY could not succeed. The judge accepted that JHY's goods were stored and handled at Heinrich's premises and that rope transactions involved prompt payments to JHY, but found that Heinrich was not shown to have suffered loss on these transactions and in fact made profits on onward sales. The court rejected the suggestion that JHY's use of Heinrich's facilities and payment flows, without more, demonstrated participation in any breach of duty or dishonest assistance. The attempt to recover from JHY on a 'user principle' basis was also rejected on the ground that the user principle is a measure of damages contingent on an established wrong involving use of property, and no such underlying wrong was proven. Accordingly, all claims against JHY Marine and Offshore Equipment Pte Ltd were dismissed.
